coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable dual-color phosphorescence Carbon-Dot@Silica material for advanced anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Hongrui
Chen, Song
Li, Min
Lu, Yongfeng
Chen, Haixin
Fang, Xiao
Qiu, Haijiang
Wang, Wensong
Jiang, Cheng
Zheng, Yuanhui
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have motivated massive attention due to their great advantages in anti-counterfeiting and optical encryption. However, the commonly used carbon-based RTP materials suffer from drawbacks of photobleaching, quenching and single phosphorescence color outputs, which obstruct their performance in high-level anti-counterfeiting techniques. Herein, we present a universal method for the fabrication of a carbon-dot@silica (CDs@SiO2) composite that possesses ultralong lifetime, high phosphorescence quantum efficiency (PQE), excellent luminescence stability, and dual-color phosphorescence emission. Taking advantage of the restriction by rigid network of SiO2 and stable covalent bonding between CDs and SiO2, the triplet excited states of CDs are stabilized. As a result, the CDs@SiO2 exhibit ultralong lifetime of 1.33 s and 0.38 s under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the phosphorescence color of CDs@SiO2 can change from cyan to yellow when the exciting wavelength switches from 254 nm to 365 nm. The obtained CDs@SiO2 also exhibit outstanding characteristics of anti-photobleaching and phosphorescence stability in an aqueous solution. Based on the unique phosphorescent properties of the CDs@SiO2, a stable and dual-color phosphorescence security label is designed for advanced information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Photostability
Carbon dots
Silica
Dual-color phosphorescence
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation process of the security labels
Mechanism for the stable luminescence of the CDs@SiO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (a) the preparation process for the CD@SiO2, (b) the proposed structure of CDs@SiO2.
(a) TEM image of CDs@SiO2, (b) FTIR spectrum of CDs@SiO2, (c) XPS pattern of CDs@SiO2, and (d–f) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, Si 2p, and O 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@SiO2, (b, c) Fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 under excitation of 254 nm and 364 nm, respectively, (d) Time-resolved phosphorescence decay and fitting curves with 254 nm and 354 nm excitation and (e) Photographs of CDs@SiO2 powders under excitation with UV lamp, and after switch-off of UV lamp.
(a) The protection mechanism of CDs by the silica matrix, and (b) the mechanism for dual-color phosphorescence emission property.

(a) Changes of phosphorescence and fluorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2 composite material under UV irradiation for up to 12 h, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of different UV exposure time, and (c) the photographs of CDs@SiO2 based security labels in the open air (row: 1), in water (row: 2).

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable dual-color phosphorescence Carbon-Dot@Silica material for advanced anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350116, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350116, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Science & Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information, 350116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Science & Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Science & Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information, 350116, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Oxford, Oxford, OX1 3QZ, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Oxford, Oxford. OX1 3QZ, United Kingdom

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350116, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have motivated massive attention due to their great advantages in anti-counterfeiting and optical encryption. However, the commonly used carbon-based RTP materials suffer from drawbacks of photobleaching, quenching and single phosphorescence color outputs, which obstruct their performance in high-level anti-counterfeiting techniques. Herein, we present a universal method for the fabrication of a carbon-dot@silica (CDs@SiO2) composite that possesses ultralong lifetime, high phosphorescence quantum efficiency (PQE), excellent luminescence stability, and dual-color phosphorescence emission. Taking advantage of the restriction by rigid network of SiO2 and stable covalent bonding between CDs and SiO2, the triplet excited states of CDs are stabilized. As a result, the CDs@SiO2 exhibit ultralong lifetime of 1.33 s and 0.38 s under excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the phosphorescence color of CDs@SiO2 can change from cyan to yellow when the exciting wavelength switches from 254 nm to 365 nm. The obtained CDs@SiO2 also exhibit outstanding characteristics of anti-photobleaching and phosphorescence stability in an aqueous solution. Based on the unique phosphorescent properties of the CDs@SiO2, a stable and dual-color phosphorescence security label is designed for advanced information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeit and shoddy products have always been a significant challenge that consumers have faced, disturbing the standard order of the market economy [1–4]. During the past decades, fake goods have been widely intertwined with our daily lives, and they can be easily found in daily consumer goods, medicines and high-tech products, etc. [5] To overcome the challenge, an effective protection solution is to mark the valuable products with anti-counterfeiting labels. The new encryption techniques for anti-counterfeiting have been rapidly developed using watermarks [6], holograms [7], luminescence [8–12], physical unclonable functions [13–17], and so on. Consequently, they have greatly enhanced real-product safety and protected the interests of consumers. Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) with a long lifetime as a luminescence encryption technique has been widely adopted in the field of anti-counterfeiting [18–25].

Generally, the hot spot of ultralong RTP materials has been focused on metal coordination complexes [26–30], organic crystals [31,32], and rare earth compounds [33]. These materials have shown great practical application prospects in information safety and anti-counterfeiting fields due to their unique optical properties. However, most of these materials suffer from either high-cost, high-toxicity, and/or complicated preparation processes. Therefore, researchers have devoted extensive effort to exploring new RTP materials in the last few years. Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging photoluminescent nanomaterial, have received significant interest owing to their superior features such as facile preparation, low cost, excellent chemical stability, good biocompatibility and tunable optical properties [34]. However, CDs alone always exhibit a relatively short lifetime and low phosphorescence quantum yield due to deficient triplet excitons and non-radiative deactivation [35]. Under prolonged or high-intensity illumination, CDs also suffer from photobleaching effect [36]. The term photobleaching refers to the loss of the luminescence properties of a system exposed for a long time under excitation source due to inducing spectral changes of the emission properties caused by photochemical oxidation process [36]. In order to achieve stable and robust RTP with a long lifetime of CDs, enormous effort has been devoted to stabilization of the triplet states of CDs. One common approach is to restrict the molecular rotation and vibration with the help of a matrix [37–44]; and the other route is to promote effective intersystem crossing (ISC) by introducing heteroatoms [43,45,46]. Recently, CDs-based RTP materials have been developed by embedding them into polyurethane [37], silica gel [38–40], polyvinyl alcohol [41], crystalline frameworks [42], boric acid [43], etc., and were applied in the field of anti-counterfeiting. In addition, heteroatom (N, B, P, F, etc.) incorporation is another effective strategy to generate and enhance the RTP of CDs [43,45,46]. However, the reported RTP CDs usually exhibit mono-color phosphorescence with serious photobleaching effect and can be easily quenched in aqueous solution, which limits their application in high-security level anti-counterfeiting in practical humid condition (e.g., in water). The realization of integration of multicolor, water-stability and photostability RTP are still remaining challenging. Meanwhile, silica materials with stable chemical properties and open porous structure have been employed as excellent host matrices to lock luminescent species of the guest (i.e., CDs) to obtain RTP properties [47]. The superb RTP phenomenon is attributed to the formed Si–O covalent bond in the whole framework [39,40]. The covalent bonds have found to be a powerful interaction force, which can better stabilize the triplet species of CDs and prevent solvents and light damage [39,47,48]. Although the synthesis of RTP CDs@SiO2 materials by embedding the pre-synthesized CDs in silica matrix has been reported [34,41,42], the large-scale synthesis of these materials through one-step reaction still remains challenge and no studies have reported the materials showing multi-color phosphorescence emission and excellent anti-photobleaching properties. Inspired by these situation and advances, the RTP properties of hybrid material obtained by introducing the silica based matrix into CDs were systematically investigated in this study.

Herein, a universal strategy is proposed to fabricate CDs@SiO2 with ultralong RTP lifetimes, high phosphorescence quantum efficiency, dual-color phosphorescence emission and extreme stability. The strategy involves simultaneous hydrolysis and crosslinking of carbon source and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and subsequent annealing treatment to generate CDs@SiO2, forming covalent bonds of Si–C between the two components. The Si–C bonds restrict the vibration and rotation of luminescent functional groups of the CDs and decrease non-radiative transitions. The lifetimes of the RTP CDs@SiO2 material reaches 1.33 s and 0.38 s under excitation of 254 and 365 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the color of phosphorescence can change from cyan to yellow by switching the exciting wavelength from 245 nm to 365 nm. The RTP CDs@SiO2 material is capable to suppress aqueous-phase quenching and UV photobleaching. Based on these convergent unique characteristics, the CDs@SiO2 material was applied in dual-color phosphorescence encryption for advanced anti-counterfeiting labels.

Glucose (AR grade, from Aladdin Reagents Shanghai Co., Ltd.), chitosan (mPa·s 50.0–800.0, from Sinopharm chemical Shanghai, China Co., Ltd.), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (K90, Mw = 360000, from Yuanye Biotechnology Shanghai Co., Ltd.), citric acid (AR grade, from Sinopharm chemical Shanghai, China Co., Ltd.), tetraethyl orthosilicate (AR grade, from Sigma-Aldrich Shanghai Co., Ltd.), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, AR grade, from Sinopharm chemical Shanghai, China Co., Ltd.), polyvinyl acetate (Mn = 30000–50000, from Sinopharm chemical Shanghai). All reagents mentioned above were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation process of the security labels
ce:section.ce:para: Polyvinyl acetate was used as the matrix for the CDs@SiO2 material. Firstly, 0.5 g of the as-prepared CDs@SiO2 powders were dispersed in 0.5 g of polyvinyl acetate and then mechanically mixed for 10 min, forming a white slurry. Then, the slurry was printed on a A4 paper by screen printing. Finally, after curing at room temperature for 10 min, the stable and dual-color phosphorescence anti-counterfeiting labels were obtained.

The PQE of the CDs@SiO2 composite material was measured by FL980 spectrometer with calibrated integrating sphere equipment. Typically, the PQY can be calculated by the following equation:
η =
ε α
=
∫
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
r e f e r e n c e
− ∫
E
s a m p l e
Where η is PQE, ε is the number of emitted phosphorescence photons of the sample that is calculated by the integrating sphere in the range of wavelength of emission spectra, α is the number of photons absorbed by the sample. L
emission is the net emission phosphorescence of the sample that fluorescence effects are eliminated by time-gate equipment. E
reference is the absorption spectrum of the reference in the integrating sphere. E
sample is the absorption spectrum of sample and reference. [49].

An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, RIGAKU Ultima IV) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) was used to characterize the crystal phases of the CDs@SiO2 composite material. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai Model G2 F20 S-TWIN) was used to examine the morphology of the CDs@SiO2 composite material. The surface elemental states of the CDs@SiO2 composite material were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG ESCALAB 250) with a monochromatized Al kα X-ray source (15 kV, 150 W, 500 nm, pass energy 20 eV). The binding energy of C1s (284.8 eV) was used for spectral calibration. The optical properties were evaluated by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu Co.). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet-iS50, Thermo Scientific Co.). The FPY was measured on the absolute quantum yield tester (Quantaurus-QY C11347-11, Hamamatsu Photonics Co.).

A universal synthetic method is developed to obtain long-lifetime RTP CDs@SiO2 composite, which possesses dual-color RTP with excellent anti-photobleaching and anti-quenching properties. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the developed synthetic method utilizes simultaneous hydrolysis and crosslinking of glucose and TEOS in the presence of ammonia solution and subsequent annealing treatment at 540 °C to generate CDs@SiO2 composite with covalent bonds of Si–C between the two components [39,40]. According to the reported literature, during the TEOS hydrolysis process, the covalent bonds of Si–C are formed between CDs and silica that act as bridges for linking the silica matrix and CDs together [39]. The CDs are thus covalently linked into the silica network, with the silica taking the role of a matrix that offers protection from environmental quenchers, such as water and oxygen. Fig. 1b shows the proposed structure of the fabricated CDs@SiO2 composite, in which the CDs with O–CO and O–H groups serve as luminescence source embedding in the SiO2 matrix linked by Si–C covalent bonds. The strong rigid network and covalent bonding can effectively restrain the intramolecular vibration, thus stabilizing the triplet excited states of the CDs with permitted RTP property [38–40,47]. Replacing glucose with other carbon sources, such as chitosan, polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), and citric acid, the RTP CDs@SiO2 composites can also be achieved (see Supplementary Fig. S1).

To confirm the morphology and structure proposed in Fig. 1b, the fabricated material was characterized with TEM, XRD spectrometer, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy and XPS. Fig. 2 a shows typical TEM images of the as-synthesized CDs@SiO2 composite. The TEM images show that the CDs@SiO2 powder is composed of large amorphous quasispherical nanoparticles and tiny black spherical dots with size of ∼5 nm well dispersed in the amorphous nanoparticles. These black dots are ascribed to CDs, while the nanoparticle matrix is assigned to SiO2 [47]. Moreover, no crystal lattices were observed during the TEM measurements, indicating the amorphous character of the as-synthesized sample [50], which is consistent with the XRD result that only exhibits a broad peak at 23°(2θ) for amorphous silica (see Supplementary Fig. S2). To verify whether the CDs have a graphitized core, the CDs@SiO2 were examined using Raman spectroscopy (see Supplementary Fig. S3).

The Raman spectrum of the synthesized CDs@SiO2 shows peaks at 830 cm−1 and 954 cm−1 corresponding to longitudinal and transversal phonon bands of SiC nanoparticles, respectively [51]. The peaks at 1297 cm−1 and 1425 cm−1 are corresponding to –CH = rocking, and CH2 = bending, respectively [52]. Surprisingly, there are no peaks belonging to D and G bands of carbon, showing that the CDs do not have any graphitized cores. In addition, the 13C NMR spectrum of CDs@SiO2 shows a weak signal at 128 ppm originated from –CH aromatic groups [53], which indicates the transformation of glucose into aromatic units trapped within the amorphous SiO2 matrix (see Supplementary Fig. S4).

The surface functional groups of the CDs@SiO2 were analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2b, the FTIR peaks at 1640 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration) and 3400 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibrations) originate from CDs, and the peaks at 450 cm−1 (Si–O rocking vibrations) and 1058 cm−1 (Si–O stretching vibrations) are ascribed to SiO2 network [39,40]. It is particularly worthy noting that the peak at 796 cm−1 (Si–C stretching vibrations) indicates that the covalent bonds between CDs and SiO2 matrix, implying the co-existence of Si–O and Si–C covalent bonds. The information presented by FTIR spectroscopy is further confirmed by the XPS analysis. Fig. 2c shows the survey XPS spectrum of the CDs@SiO2. The XPS peaks of C, Si and O elements are observed, showing that the composite mainly consists of C, Si and O elements, which is in good agreement with the results of elemental mapping (see Supplementary Fig. S5). Specifically, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, Si 2p and O 1s are exhibited in Fig. 2d–f. The high-resolution XPS C 1s spectra show the existence of C–Si (284.0 eV), CC/C–C 284.8 eV), C–O (285.6 eV) and O–CO (286.3 eV) bonds (Fig. 2d) [54,55]. Fig. 2e displays the high-resolution Si 2p XPS spectrum, where three different formats of silicon species, i.e., Si–C (102.7 eV), Si–O (103.4 eV) and SiO2 gel (104.0 eV) are observed [39]. Fig. 2f shows the high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum of the CDs@SiO2. There are two different types of oxygen species with XPS peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.9 eV corresponding to the oxygen in CO and Si–O groups, respectively. Taken together, we conclude that CDs were embedded in the silica matrix linked by Si–C covalent bonds.

The optical properties of the CDs@SiO2 were systematically investigated. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs@SiO2 displays an absorption peak at 225 nm and a broad absorption band spanning from around 280 to 500 nm (Fig. 3 a), which are assigned to the π→π* transition of the CC bond and n→π* transition of the CO groups, respectively [56]. Furthermore, the CDs@SiO2 displayed a typical excitation-dependent fluorescence and phosphorescence properties between the excitation wavelength of 254 and 374 nm (see Supplementary Fig. S6), which indicates the presence of multi-fluorescence and phosphorescence centers in the material. The phosphorescence emission peak red-shifts and their intensity decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength (Fig. S6). Fig. 3b and c shows the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 excited at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. As presented in Fig. 3b, the CDs@SiO2 composite shows fluorescent emission at 325 nm and phosphorescent emission at 460 nm when excited at 254 nm. Significantly, the presence of several shoulder peaks (at 340 nm, 360 nm and 370 nm) in the fluorescence emission peak confirms that there are multiple emission centers in the CDs@SiO2 composite. A similar phenomenon was also observed in the phosphorescence spectrum under excitation at 254 nm (Fig. 3b), which implies multi-phosphorescence centers in the CDs@SiO2 composite. Fig. 3c shows that the CDs@SiO2 composite displays blue fluorescence emission at 470 nm and yellow RTP emission at 525 nm with a half-peak width of ∼150 nm at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Based on the above results, possible multicolor RTP origins of CDs@SiO2 could be ascribed to the synergy between surface groups and rigid matrix. For embedded CDs, CO/CC groups can form various conjugation structures during the calcining process and act as emitting centers with distinct energy gaps [20]. The CO/CC groups can promote spin-orbit coupling and the ISC process. The amorphous silica is used as the matrix to fix the CDs and stabilize the excited triplet species. Upon excitation with different energy photons, some emitting centers could prevail in the RTP process and consequently display dual colors.

The fluorescence quantum efficiency (FQE) and PQE are the key parameters for luminescent materials. The FQE and PQE of the CDs@SiO2 composite were measured to be 37.4%, 16.7% under 254-nm light excitation and 20.4%, 1.3% under 365-nm light excitation, respectively. Fig. 3d shows the afterglow decay spectra of CDs@SiO2 under 254-nm and 365-nm excitation, which can be fitted with a tri-exponential function using the following equation:
(1)
τ
a v g
= ∑
α i
τ i 2
/ ∑
α i
τ i
Where τ avg is the average lifetime, τ
i is the lifetime of component i and α
i its intensity contribution to the fluorescence decay, respectively. The average lifetimes was estimated to be 1.33 s and 0.38 s with excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Fig. 3e displays the photographs of CDs@SiO2 under 254-nm and 365-nm excitation and after the removal of the excitation sources. The CDs@SiO2 composite exhibits cyan RTP emission after switching off the 254-nm lamp, and the phosphorescence lasts for 10 s (see the top panel of Fig. 3e and Supplementary Video 1.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2
2
Multimedia component 2

Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110827). The CDs@SiO2 powders exhibit bright blue-greenish emission under UV lamp (365-nm) and yellow phosphorescence that can last for 3 s after switching off the UV irradiation (see the bottom panel of Fig. 3e and Supplementary Video 2

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 3
3
Multimedia component 3

Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110827). The same phenomenon can also be observed in aqueous solution (see Supplementary Fig. S7). The multi-phosphorescence centers of CDs@SiO2 endow the material excitation wavelength-dependent phosphorescent property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the stable luminescence of the CDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above discussion, the possible mechanism for realizing such ultralong lifetimes, high quantum efficiency and dual-color high stability RTP CDs@SiO2 is proposed. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 4 , during the calcination process, the TEOS undergoes hydrolysis and crosslinking reactions, eventually forming an amorphous SiO2 network, and the glucose tends to self-polymerization and forms CDs. Under high-temperature conditions, the synthesized CDs as luminescence centers with CO and O–H functional groups were embedded into the silica matrix through Si–O and Si–C covalent bonds. The covalent bonds formed within the silica framework can protect CDs from the quenchers (i.e., H2O and O2) from the environment [47], as illustrated in Fig. 4a. To investigate the effects of quenchers on luminescence performance and lifetime, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra and phosphorescence lifetime of the CDs@SiO2 powder in water and in the absence of oxygen (i.e., under vacuum) were measured at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S8). The fluorescence peak splits into two peaks in water compared to the situation under vacuum, while the phosphorescence profiles shows little change in the two scenarios The fitting details of the phosphorescence decay curves in air, in water and under vacuum were listed in Supplementary Table S1. There is no significant change in the average phosphorescence lifetime of the CDs@SiO2 powder in the three different environments, indicating that majority of the CDs were embedded in the amorphous silica. It should be noted that there are still a small amount of CDs on the surface of the silica. The photogenerated carriers of these surface CDs can react with oxygen or hydroxyl groups originating from the adsorbed water molecules, which results in the slight decrease of the average phosphorescence lifetime of the CDs@SiO2 powder. From Table S1, it is found that water molecules have a more negative effect on the phosphorescence lifetime than oxygen molecules. Furthermore, the silica network stabilizes and promotes the unstable triplet excited states, forming multiple emissive centers. The self-polymerization process generates abundant CC and CO relevant moieties on the CD surface, which leads to the multi-triplet state with different color phosphorescence emissions. Consequently, multiple triplet excited states are formed, resulting in excitation wavelength-dependent on phosphorescence property for the CDs@SiO2 composite material (Fig. 4b).

Benefiting from the unique dual-color phosphorescence features, the CDs@SiO2 composite was explored for multilevel-encryption application. The excellent water- and photo-stability is an essential property of luminous anti-counterfeiting labels for their practical applications. To investigate the anti-photobleaching property of the CDs@SiO2 composite, the material was irradiated with UV light (365 nm, 6 W) for a duration up to 12 h, and the changes of the fluorescent intensity and peaks were monitored at different time points (Fig. 5 a–b). As shown in Fig. 5a, the small fluctuation of fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity was observed due to the experimental error. The corresponding photographs of the CDs@SiO2 composite with different UV exposure (365 nm) time were shown in Supplementary Fig. S9. Fig. 5b exhibits the time-dependent fluorescence spectra of the CDs@SiO2 composite during the continuous UV irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp (6 W). The results show no variation in the profile of the fluorescent spectra under UV exposure, revealing no generation of new emission centers. The photostability studies reflected the excellent resistance of the CDs@SiO2 composite to photobleaching. The outstanding photostability and unique excitation-dependent phosphorescence features provide a great potential of the CDs@SiO2 composite in the information encryption field. As a proof-of-concept, the fabricated composite was used as a luminescent ink for advanced anti-counterfeiting applications. To examine the waterproof and encryption performances, the prepared security labels are immersed into the aqueous solution and excited under 254- and 365- nm UV light, respectively. Fig. 5c shows a banyan tree pattern and intaglio printing printed letters of “FZU NANO PHOTONICS” on A4 paper using the CDs@SiO2 based ink. Cyan and white phosphorescence can be observed after irradiation with 254 and 365 nm light. Impressively, the labels still exhibit stable phosphorescence emission after immersion in aqueous solution for 1 h (bottom panel of Fig. 5c). It is believed that the formation of Si–O and Si–C covalent bonds stabilize the excited triplet states of CDs and prevent water and light damage. The stable framework of SiO2 could tightly accommodate CDs. The highly rigid network with spatial restriction can effectively limit the intramolecular rotation and vibration and isolate surrounding oxygen and water, thus the ultralong phosphorescence of the water-dispersed CDs@SiO2 can be achieved. These characteristics permit application of the developed security labels in humid environment. The joint excellent properties of anti-photobleaching, photo-stimulated dual-color phosphorescence and water stability within the CDs@SiO2 phosphor have endow its multi-dimensional information encryption to be mimicked or forged by outlaws, making it a promising strategy for high-level anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, we have developed a universal synthetic method for the fabrication of RTP of CDs@SiO2 composite materials, in which CDs were incorporated into a rigid silica network through the hydrolysis and condensation reactions of TEOS. The synthesized CDs@SiO2 exhibit exceptionally long RTP lifetimes of 1.33 s and 0.38 s under 254 and 365 nm excitation, respectively. In addition, it owns a high PQE of 16.7%. Furthermore, the formation of covalent bonds between CDs and silica may play a unique role in stable and dual-color RTP emission and act by rigidifying the excited triplet species, promoting and enhancing the electrons on triplet state to undergo radiative recombination and suppressing nonradiative transitions. Based on these unique characteristics, we have developed a series of innovative security labels. These security labels show excitation-wavelength-dependent phosphorescence emissions, fantastic anti-photobleaching and brilliant anti-quenching properties. The findings offer bright prospects for high-level anti-counterfeiting.

Hongrui Cheng: Conceived the idea, Designed experiments and synthesized material, Wrote the manuscript. Song Chen: Conceived the idea, Designed experiments and synthesized material, Wrote the manuscript. Min Li: Conceived the idea, Formal analysis. Yongfeng Lu: Formal analysis. Haixin Chen: Formal analysis. Xiao Fang: Formal analysis. Haijiang Qiu: Done transmission and fluorescent spectra test. Wensong Wang: Done transmission and fluorescent spectra test. Cheng Jiang: Wrote the manuscript. Yuanhui Zheng: Conceived the idea, Was involved in programming, and, Supervision, Wrote the manuscript, All authors reviewed it.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Recruitment Program of Global Experts, National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62175033 and 61775040), Fujian Science & Technology Innovation Laboratory for Optoelectronic Information (Grant No. 2021ZZ126), the Hundred-Talent Project of Fujian, and Fuzhou University for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data to this article:

The photographs of CD@SiO2 powders fabricated with different carbon sources with 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamps on and off; XRD pattern, Raman spectrum, 13C NMR spectrum of CDs@SiO2 powder; EDS results of the CDs@SiO2 powder; excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 powder; fluorescence and phosphorescence emission of CDs@SiO2 powder in aqueous solution under 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamp on and off; pictures of fluorescence and phosphorescence emission of CDs@SiO2 powder with different UV exposure times (PDF).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design, fabrication and characterization of Computer Generated Holograms for anti-counterfeiting applications using OAM beams as light decoders
